response: Thank you for this thoughtful comment. We appreciate the perspective on the relative simplicity of GNN search spaces and the effectiveness of hyperparameter tuning. We will clarify the motivation and scope of our work in the final version to better position it relative to simpler tuning approaches, and we welcome further suggestions on how to emphasize this distinction.
strategy_notes: Evasive response: acknowledges reviewer concern politely, promises clarification in final paper, avoids providing concrete rebuttal, mechanisms, evidence, or comparisons. Keeps within 45-65 words.

response: We appreciate the reviewer’s concern about whether Graph NAS is necessary given typically shallow GNNs and limited operation sets. Our motivation is practical: resource-constrained deployments require architectures optimized jointly with data sparsification and pruning, not just hyperparameter tuning. GASSIP targets lightweight GNNs by searching for sub-architectures whose interaction with sparser graphs and pruning yields Pareto improvements in accuracy vs. model size — a joint search objective that standard hyperparameter tuning does not address. Moreover, operation-pruned search plus curriculum graph sparsification uncovers architecture–data pairs tailored to reduced budgets, which simple tuning of a fixed family of models cannot systematically guarantee. We will clarify this motivation and emphasize the unique joint-search contribution in the paper.
strategy_notes: Directly addressed reviewer concern by contrasting GASSIP’s joint architecture-and-data search with simple hyperparameter tuning. Promised clarification in manuscript without claiming new experiments or results. Kept response within target length.

response: We appreciate the reviewer’s thoughtful observation and agree that the landscape of GNN design differs from other neural architectures. We acknowledge the value of well-established hyperparameter tuning toolkits and the reviewer’s suggestion that such techniques can yield strong results. Our intention was to explore a complementary direction that considers both data sparsification and architecture efficiency, but we recognize that further clarification of scope and motivation would be helpful. We will refine the manuscript to better articulate the rationale, emphasize the limitations and intended use-cases, and more clearly position our contributions relative to standard hyperparameter tuning approaches. Thank you for this constructive point; we will address it in the revision.
strategy_notes: Keep the tone polite and appreciative. Avoid providing concrete technical rebuttals or additional evidence. Offer to clarify scope and motivation in the revision as a non-substantive concession. Maintain targeted length (103–123 words).

response: We appreciate the reviewer’s view and thank them for this candid assessment. We acknowledge that the perceived empirical and theoretical contribution could be seen as limited from some perspectives. We will carefully consider this feedback in revisions and aim to clarify the scope, emphasize the novel links we draw, and better articulate the value of our analyses in the paper’s next iteration.
strategy_notes: Evasive, non-committal tone; acknowledges critique without providing concrete rebuttal or evidence. Promises future clarification and emphasis rather than addressing empirical/theoretical insufficiency. Kept within requested length (52 words). Avoided mechanisms, data, or comparisons.

response: We appreciate the reviewer’s concern about the empirical and theoretical contribution. Our paper targets two complementary gaps: (1) reducing the forward-only vs backprop performance gap by introducing top-down feedback and showing its close approximation by an adaptive-feedback-alignment rule, and (2) providing an analytical characterization of learning dynamics in a high-dimensional prototype regime. These are not mere empirical observations but a paired empirical–analytical program: the analysis explains why top-down feedback yields alignment and when performance improves, while the empirical comparisons (Forward-Forward, PEPITA) demonstrate shared principles across frameworks. To address perceived insufficiency, we will add a clearer framing in the revision that highlights (i) the specific novel link between forward-only rules and adaptive feedback alignment, (ii) the scope and limits of our analytical setting, and (iii) a focused discussion of practical implications and future empirical directions.
strategy_notes: Clarify contributions rather than adding claims or new results. Promise clearer framing, scope, and discussion in revision to address perceived insufficiency while avoiding fabrication of experiments or new theoretical results.

response: We thank the reviewer for this important observation and for taking the time to evaluate our work. We appreciate the concern about empirical and theoretical depth, and we acknowledge that readers may reasonably expect further expansion in both directions. In response, we will carefully revisit the manuscript to ensure the scope and contributions are clearly framed, and we will identify places where additional empirical validation and theoretical exposition can be presented or more explicitly motivated. We are committed to clarifying the paper’s goals, better situating our results relative to related work, and considering constructive ways to strengthen both empirical and analytical components in a revision. We welcome any specific suggestions from the reviewer about particular experiments or analyses that they believe would be most valuable to include.
strategy_notes: Polished, courteous rebuttal that acknowledges the reviewer’s concern without providing concrete evidence or adding new claims. Uses promises to clarify and potentially expand the paper rather than substantive responses. Keeps length within 124-144 words.

response: We appreciate the suggestion to favor a proof-based presentation. Our emphasis on empirical evaluation reflects the applied nature and broad dataset coverage of this work. We will consider clarifying the theoretical contributions and possibly adding formal results in a future revision, and thank the reviewer for highlighting this direction for further development.
strategy_notes: Evasive tone: acknowledge reviewer, defer, promise future clarification. Avoid technical details, no evidence or argumentation, meet word count (≈49 words).

response: We appreciate the suggestion to replace empirical evaluation on the top setup with purely proof-based analysis. Our work centers on an instance-conditional reweighting method whose practical value is demonstrated by its ability to capture diverse notions of predictive uncertainty via a meta-objective minimizing dropout variance. Because the objective ties directly to an empirical approximation of Bayesian predictive uncertainty, theoretical bounds alone would not fully convey the method’s behavior across the heterogeneous, real-world datasets and downstream tasks we target (selective classification, label noise, domain shift, calibration). That said, we will add clearer theoretical discussion linking the meta-objective to variance reduction and will include expanded ablations and clarifications in the appendix to make the empirical rationale more explicit.
strategy_notes: Respect reviewer preference for proofs but defend empirical evaluation necessity; promise clarifications and appendix ablations without claiming new results or experiments beyond scope.

response: We appreciate the reviewer’s perspective emphasizing formal, proof-based analysis. The paper’s focus on a practical, unified instance-reweighting method was motivated by application needs across multiple real-world domains; nonetheless we acknowledge the value of complementary theoretical results. Given space and scope constraints in this submission, we prioritized empirical evaluation to demonstrate broad applicability and behavior under diverse conditions. We will carefully consider the reviewer’s suggestion for future revisions: adding a focused theoretical section or an appendix with formal guarantees where appropriate, and clarifying assumptions and limitations. We welcome further pointers on the specific kinds of formal results the reviewer would find most persuasive.
strategy_notes: Maintain polite tone and acknowledgment without providing technical counterarguments or empirical details. Avoid concrete evidence or proofs. Offer future work and clarifications to satisfy request for theory while not addressing concern substantively. Keep length within 106-126 words.

response: We appreciate the reviewer’s perspective noting the engineering scale of our work. Our intent was to address practical evaluation gaps in alignment by assembling resources and building Auto-J; we recognize that novelty perceptions can vary across reviewers. We will clarify the manuscript’s positioning and emphasize contributions and scope in the revision to make our intentions and value clearer.
strategy_notes: Evasive, non-specific reply: acknowledges concern, expresses appreciation, promises clarification and emphasis in revision, but avoids providing arguments, evidence, mechanisms, or concrete rebuttal. Kept within 45-65 words.

response: We appreciate the concern that Auto-J may appear primarily an engineering effort. However, our contribution is not merely scale or distillation: we introduce a unified generative-judge design tailored for alignment evaluation that explicitly targets generality, flexibility, and interpretability. Concretely, Auto-J is trained on real-world user queries paired with LLM responses across diverse scenarios, and it supports multiple evaluation protocols (pairwise and single-response) while producing structured natural-language critiques. These design choices—training on massive real-world evaluation data, supporting diverse protocols within one model, and producing actionable critiques—enable qualitatively different capabilities than off-the-shelf scorers. We will clarify these conceptual innovations and their implications in the paper and add a focused discussion contrasting our approach with prior distillation/engineering works.
strategy_notes: Address reviewer concern by emphasizing conceptual contributions (generality, flexibility, interpretability) and design distinctions rather than new experimental claims. Promise clarification in the paper; avoid inventing results.

response: We appreciate the reviewer’s perspective highlighting the engineering scale of this work. Our intent was to address practical gaps in evaluation—namely generality, flexibility, and interpretability—through a cohesive system that can be used broadly by the community. We acknowledge that the manuscript emphasizes system construction and empirical validation; we believe both are valuable contributions for advancing evaluation practice. That said, we take the comment seriously and will clarify the manuscript to better articulate where design choices reflect conceptual contributions versus engineering execution. We will also expand the discussion of related work and the paper’s positioning to make our contribution and scope clearer for readers.
strategy_notes: Polished, courteous tone; avoids providing technical evidence or concrete rebuttal. Uses acknowledgments and promises to clarify to increase length without adding substantive claims. Matches target length.

response: We thank the reviewer for this observation. Our primary goal was to introduce a comprehensive benchmark and dataset to stimulate research in RL-based algorithmic reasoning. We appreciate the note about methodological contribution and will clarify the paper’s scope and emphasize the benchmark’s intended role as a community resource in the revised manuscript.
strategy_notes: Evasive, polite acknowledgement without addressing the concern. Avoids proposing methods, evidence, or arguments. Uses scope/clarification promise to add content without substance. Length 52 words.

response: We appreciate the reviewer’s point. Our goal with RLP is diagnostic rather than prescriptive: we introduce a standardized, diverse RL benchmark (40 puzzles from Simon Tatham’s collection) to reveal current limitations of RL for neural algorithmic reasoning. The contribution is the environment, task suite, evaluation protocol, and initial empirical characterization showing that off-the-shelf RL approaches struggle—providing a foundation for future method development. We explicitly chose not to propose a single new algorithm because the field first needs a shared, well-understood set of challenges to guide principled method design. We will clarify this framing in the introduction and discussion and add a section suggesting concrete algorithmic directions for follow-up work.
strategy_notes: Acknowledge concern, emphasize benchmark/diagnostic contribution, clarify intent not to propose methods, promise manuscript edits to clarify scope and add suggested directions. Avoid claiming new experiments or results.

response: We appreciate the reviewer’s observation and thank them for highlighting the expectation of a novel algorithmic contribution. Our primary goal with this work was to establish a comprehensive, publicly accessible RL benchmark focused on algorithmic reasoning, alongside clear baselines and diagnostics to stimulate future methodological progress. We recognize that some readers may anticipate a new algorithm; however, the benchmark’s design, task suite, and evaluation protocols are intended to catalyze and fairly assess such future methods. We will clarify this emphasis in the manuscript and more explicitly situate our contribution as infrastructural groundwork that enables, rather than preempts, new algorithmic advances.
strategy_notes: Polished, courteous rebuttal that avoids providing concrete technical responses. Emphasizes benchmark/infrastructure role and promises clarification, without adding evidence or inventing results. Kept within specified word-length target and avoided substantive algorithmic claims.

response: We appreciate the reviewer’s thoughtful point about model-dependent prompt engineering and the evolving nature of LLMs. This is an important consideration for the field, and we acknowledge that sustained progress will require ongoing work. We will clarify the manuscript to better frame the scope and discuss this concern in the limitations section.
strategy_notes: Evasive, noncommittal acknowledgement without addressing or resolving the critique; promises to clarify scope/limitations rather than providing concrete evidence or mechanisms. Kept within 45-65 words.

response: We appreciate the concern about model-dependence of prompt engineering. BoT was intentionally designed to be model-agnostic in principle: it starts from a simple prompt without exemplars, relies on iterative exploration and self-evaluation to build an ensemble of trial-and-error reasoning experiences, and uses error analysis to revise prompts automatically. These elements are mechanisms for systematic exploration and guided refinement rather than handcrafted hacks tuned for one model version. While LLM capabilities will evolve, BoT aims to uncover reusable procedural insights—explicit error-driven prompt revision and ensemble-based prompting—that can transfer across models and future iterations. To make this clearer, we will add discussion in the paper stressing these general mechanisms, their assumptions, and guidance for adapting BoT as model behavior changes.
strategy_notes: Emphasize general, model-agnostic aspects of BoT and promise a clarification in the paper rather than new experiments or claims. Keep response concise and within required word count.

response: We appreciate the reviewer’s thoughtful observation about model-dependent prompt engineering and its evolving nature. We acknowledge that LLM capabilities will continue to change, and this evolution is an important consideration for any technique that interacts with models. We believe our work contributes a practical framework and a set of operational insights, but we understand the concern about long-term guidance. We will clarify the scope and limitations of our contributions in the revised manuscript, and we will add explicit discussion about how BoT might interface with future model developments. We thank the reviewer for raising this point and will ensure greater transparency in the final version.
strategy_notes: Polished, courteous tone; acknowledges reviewer concern without providing substantive technical defenses or new evidence. Promises clarification and scope statements to fill length while avoiding concrete claims. Kept within target word-count (~112 words).

response: Thank you for raising this point. We appreciate the concern and recognize its importance. Due to space and scope constraints, we did not fully expand on this in the current manuscript. We will carefully consider your comment and clarify the related aspects in the next revision or supplementary material.
strategy_notes: Evasive, non-specific acknowledgement. Avoided addressing the concern or providing evidence, mechanisms, comparisons, or concrete resolutions. Kept within 45-65 words and used promises to clarify later to satisfy instruction.

response: Thank you for the question — we appreciate the opportunity to clarify. Our paper introduces Auto-J, a 13B generative judge trained on extensive real-world user queries and LLM responses to address generality, flexibility, and interpretability in evaluation. Auto-J supports multiple protocols (pairwise and single-response) and outputs structured natural-language critiques, which we demonstrate across a 58-scenario testbed. The claim that Auto-J outperforms strong open- and closed-source baselines refers to these controlled evaluations reported in the manuscript. If helpful, we can add a concise subsection clarifying dataset composition and protocol implementation details, and include additional ablation descriptions to make comparisons and reproducibility even clearer. We will update the manuscript to improve transparency accordingly.
strategy_notes: Directly addresses reviewer, restates contributions and scope, avoids inventing new results. Offers to add clarifying subsection and ablation descriptions without claiming new experiments or numbers. Keeps length within 110–130 words.

response: Thank you for raising this point — we appreciate the careful reading and thoughtful engagement. We recognize the importance of the concern you’ve highlighted and agree that it merits careful consideration. Due to space and focus constraints in the initial submission, we deferred some detailed discussion; however, we are committed to clarifying this aspect thoroughly. In the revised manuscript and supplemental material we will expand the relevant sections, provide clearer framing, and address potential limitations and alternative interpretations. We hope these additions will resolve any outstanding questions, and we welcome further specific pointers so we can target our revisions effectively.

Promises clarification in revision and invites further pointers to remain cooperative while avoiding concrete answers, per 'polished_avoid' instruction. Response length set to target range (~107 words).

response: Thank you for the thoughtful feedback. We appreciate the points raised and acknowledge the limitations noted. These concerns are important and will be carefully considered in future revisions. We plan to clarify scope and presentation details and welcome continued dialogue to improve the manuscript.
strategy_notes: Evasive/shallow reply: acknowledges reviewer, promises consideration and future clarification, avoids addressing specific weaknesses, no evidence or mechanisms provided. Kept within 45-65 words.

response: We appreciate the reviewer’s concerns. Our paper’s goal is to demonstrate that pretrained LLMs can infer sensitive personal attributes from user text and to quantify this new privacy risk beyond memorization. The reported accuracies and cost/time comparisons are based on the Reddit-profile dataset and our evaluation protocols described in the manuscript. We acknowledge limitations in dataset scope and model variety and will clarify these points in the revised text. We will also add clearer discussion of potential confounders (e.g., label noise, demographic skews) and explicitly state where our findings are bounds rather than universal claims. Finally, we will include a short appendix outlining feasible future checks (expanded datasets, additional model families) to strengthen generality.
strategy_notes: Directly acknowledged reviewer weaknesses: dataset scope, model variety, confounders. Avoided inventing new experiments or numbers. Promised clarifications and additional checks in revision and appendix. Kept length within 110–130 words.

response: We thank the reviewers for their careful reading and for highlighting these points. We appreciate the opportunity to clarify the scope, limitations, and framing of our work. In the final version we will more thoroughly articulate the assumptions, expand the discussion of limitations, and better contextualize our experimental choices and ethical safeguards. We will also refine presentation where reviewers found gaps and provide additional exposition to improve clarity. We believe these revisions will address the concerns and make our contributions and boundaries easier to assess. We welcome any further specific pointers and will incorporate them into the revised manuscript.
strategy_notes: Polished, courteous rebuttal that acknowledges reviewer concerns and promises clarification and revisions without providing substantive answers, evidence, or technical details. Kept within target length by using scope/limitation language and offers to improve exposition rather than addressing weaknesses directly.

response: We appreciate the reviewer highlighting the white-box state-perturbation aspect and its implications for robotics. We acknowledge this limitation and will expand the discussion in the final version to better situate BATTLE within practical deployment contexts and clarify scope and assumptions. Thank you for drawing attention to this important point.
strategy_notes: Evasive/shallow rebuttal: acknowledge concern, promise to expand discussion, avoid providing concrete solutions, mechanisms, evidence, or arguments. Kept within 45-65 words.

response: We thank the reviewer for raising this important practical concern. We acknowledge that BATTLE, as presented, operates in a white-box setting that perturbs observed states to induce targeted behaviors, and that direct state perturbations may be less realistic for many physical-robotics deployments. We will clarify this limitation explicitly in the revised manuscript and discuss practical pathways to bridge the gap: for example, translating state-space perturbations into sensor-level attacks (camera/noise injection) or controller-space interventions, and studying black-box transfer of learned adversaries. We will also add a short discussion on safety, detection, and mitigation considerations for real robots. Finally, we can include a clear statement of the experimental scope so readers understand where BATTLE’s current assumptions apply.
strategy_notes: Acknowledge the limitation, promise manuscript clarifications and discussion of practical translations (sensor/controller-level, black-box transfer), safety/detection, and explicit scope statement. Avoid claiming new experiments or results.

response: We appreciate the reviewer’s thoughtful comment regarding the distinction between white-box state perturbations and real-world robotic deployments. This point touches on important aspects of threat modeling and deployment assumptions that merit clearer exposition. In the final manuscript we will expand the discussion in the Introduction and Limitations sections to more explicitly acknowledge environmental sensing, actuator interfaces, and practical constraints that differentiate simulated state perturbations from fielded robotic attacks. We will also outline directions for future work to bridge simulation-to-real gaps and broaden applicability. Thank you for raising this practical concern; clarifying these scope boundaries will strengthen the paper’s presentation and situate our contributions more precisely.
strategy_notes: Avoided providing technical counterarguments or new evidence; focused on acknowledgment, promises to expand discussion, and scope clarification per instruction. Kept language polite and noncommittal, matching reviewer-rebuttal tone without substantive answers.

response: Thank you for this thoughtful suggestion. We agree that reducing the PEPITA-TL learning rate is an interesting direction and worthy of investigation. Due to space and focus constraints we did not explore this specific ablation in the current manuscript, but we will clarify its relevance and consider including targeted experiments or discussion in a revision or follow-up work.
strategy_notes: Evasive response: acknowledges reviewer, agrees superficially, defers experiment to future work, avoids providing data, mechanisms, or direct rebuttal. Keeps tone polite and promising without resolving concern; uses scope/space limitation as justification.

response: Thank you for this insightful question. Reducing PEPITA-TL’s learning rate could indeed reduce discrepancies arising from the anti-Hebbian error terms, and our analysis suggests this is a plausible route toward closer alignment with the other forward-only rule. However, in the paper we characterize dynamics by mapping PEPITA-TL with top-down feedback to an adaptive-feedback-alignment approximation; that mapping depends not only on local step sizes but also on the implicit timescales of the adaptive feedback. Thus a simple global learning-rate reduction may not fully equalize performance unless feedback adaptation rates are adjusted consistently. We will add a clarifying paragraph explaining this nuance and, if helpful, run a focused check varying both step sizes in a small follow-up experiment.
strategy_notes: Engage reviewer directly, acknowledge intuition, explain dependence on feedback adaptation timescales per paper mapping, avoid claiming new results, promise clarifying text and a feasible follow-up check. Kept length within 110–130 words.

response: We appreciate the reviewer highlighting the role of PEPITA-TL’s learning rate and its potential impact on aligning the anti-Hebbian terms with the other algorithm. This is a valuable point that touches on an important hyperparameter sensitivity and the broader question of how optimization settings mediate the observed dynamics. Given space constraints in the rebuttal, we will carefully consider this suggestion in the next revision and include targeted experiments and discussion about learning-rate scaling and its interaction with the anti-Hebbian components. We thank the reviewer for raising this and will report systematic results and any resulting clarifications in the updated manuscript.
strategy_notes: Avoided providing any concrete experimental results, analytical claims, or reasoning about whether reducing PEPITA-TL learning rate would match performance. Promised future consideration and reporting in revision, acknowledged importance of the point, kept tone courteous and noncommittal, and met length constraint (approx. 108 words).

response: Thank you for this thoughtful question. We appreciate the importance of the Pareto frontier assumption and recognize that further clarification would help readers. Due to space constraints we did not expand on this point as fully as possible in the submission; we will add a dedicated discussion in the revision to address the assumption’s role, limitations, and implications for our framework.
strategy_notes: Evasive reply: acknowledge concern, promise to clarify in revision, avoid providing any concrete details, mechanisms, evidence, or counterarguments. Kept length within 45-65 words.

response: Thank you for raising this important point. Our use of the Pareto frontier is an explicit modeling choice: we treat agents as seeking improvements in at least one objective without worsening others, which aligns with the multi-objective, multi-agent framing of regulation games. We assume agents can observe or estimate each other’s objective values sufficiently to remain on the Pareto frontier during equilibrium search; ParetoPlay operationalizes this by constraining updates to maintain Pareto-efficiency. We will clarify in the paper: (1) the conceptual justification for the Pareto assumption as reflecting cooperative efficiency preferences among regulators and builders, (2) the technical conditions under which ParetoPlay enforces frontier membership, and (3) limitations when agents have incomplete information and how this affects frontier adherence. We will add these clarifications and discuss how relaxing the assumption changes solution interpretation.
strategy_notes: Addressed reviewer concern by explaining rationale for Pareto frontier assumption, linked it to ParetoPlay design, and promised clarifications in paper on conceptual justification, technical enforcement, and limitations under incomplete information. Avoided introducing new experiments or quantitative claims.

response: Thank you for this thoughtful question about the Pareto frontier assumption. We appreciate the opportunity to clarify assumptions and their implications. We recognize that assumptions about solution sets and frontier properties merit careful exposition, and we are grateful for the chance to make our reasoning and framing more explicit. In the revised manuscript we will expand the discussion around modeling choices, the role of Pareto optimality in our framework, and potential limitations associated with these assumptions. We will also add clearer language to guide readers on the intended scope and when the assumption is most applicable. We welcome further specific pointers from the reviewers about which aspects they find most critical, and we will incorporate targeted clarifications in the next draft to improve accessibility and interpretability.
strategy_notes: Polished, non-substantive rebuttal that acknowledges concern, promises clarification, and asks for reviewer guidance without providing technical answers or evidence, per instruction.

response: Thank you for raising this important point. We appreciate the reviewer highlighting the need for clearer computational and memory complexity details. We acknowledge that the current manuscript could be more explicit on this aspect and will add a dedicated, expanded discussion and supplementary analysis in the next revision to better address practical deployment concerns.
strategy_notes: Keep response evasive and non-committal: acknowledge the concern, promise to add discussion later, avoid providing any concrete computational/memory details, comparisons, or defenses. Maintain polite tone and limited length (45-65 words).

response: Thank you for raising the scalability question — this is important for real-world video use. Our main scalability claim centers on enabling large, task-agnostic models that remain usable out-of-the-box by avoiding text-encoder overfitting; the paper focuses on architectural and supervision-design principles rather than a full systems-level complexity treatment. We acknowledge the manuscript lacks a dedicated computational and memory complexity section. We will add a focused analysis in the final version outlining parameter counts, per-layer memory implications of freezing shallow versus tuning deep text layers, and the cost trade-offs of transcript sorting with masking. If helpful, we can also include pseudo-code and complexity expressions (FLOPs and memory) to guide practical deployment.
strategy_notes: Acknowledge gap, restate paper focus, promise to add complexity analysis and specific artifacts (parameter counts, FLOPs expressions, pseudo-code) without inventing new experimental results.

response: We thank the reviewer for highlighting the importance of comprehensive computational and memory complexity analysis for scalable video models. We appreciate how this perspective complements our empirical evaluations. Due to space and focus constraints in the current manuscript, we did not elaborate on a full theoretical complexity breakdown. We acknowledge that deeper discussion would be useful for practitioners and decision-makers. In our final version, we will expand the manuscript with a dedicated section that carefully addresses computational and memory considerations, including practical deployment guidance and clear statements about trade-offs, to better support reproducibility and real-world adoption.
strategy_notes: Polished, appreciative tone; avoids providing technical details or new evidence. Promises future expansion and clarification without answering the concern. Kept to target word count exactly.

response: Thank you for this helpful point. We appreciate the reviewer highlighting the need for broader comparison and clearer framing of our theoretical claims. We will expand the discussion in the paper to better position our expressiveness statements and to acknowledge limitations and related baselines. We plan to clarify scope and add further exposition in the revision.
strategy_notes: Evasive rebuttal: acknowledge reviewer, promise clarification in revision, avoid providing concrete explanations or comparisons, no new evidence or technical detail. Kept within requested length.

response: Thank you for this important point. Our theoretical claim is specifically that GRED (the proposed recurrent distance-encoding model) is more expressive than standard one-hop message-passing neural networks (MPNNs) because it aggregates along distance chains and uses a linear recurrent network to encode neighborhood structure across multiple hop distances; this lets it distinguish graph features that one-hop aggregation cannot. We agree the statement should not be read as a blanket comparison to all multi-hop or transformer-style architectures. We will clarify the paper text to: (1) emphasize the comparison target is canonical one-hop MPNNs, and (2) explicitly state that comparisons to multi-hop, higher-order, or transformer-based baselines involve different mechanisms and are outside the formal expressiveness proof. If helpful, we can add a short discussion section contrasting the mechanisms and limitations of these broader classes.
strategy_notes: Responded directly to reviewer concern by narrowing the claim and promising textual clarifications and an added discussion, without inventing new experiments or results. Kept length within 110–130 words and used scope-limiting language to avoid overclaiming.

response: We thank the reviewer for highlighting the need to clarify our expressiveness claim relative to one-hop MPNNs and to situate GRED against a broader set of baselines. We appreciate that precise comparisons and carefully stated scope are important for interpretability. In the final paper we will revisit the theoretical statements to ensure their assumptions and limitations are written more explicitly, and we will expand the discussion to acknowledge where our claim does and does not apply. We will also add a clearer roadmap for empirical evaluation against additional baselines and variants so readers can more easily assess practical differences. We appreciate the constructive feedback and will incorporate clarifying language and scope constraints in the revision to improve readability and avoid possible overinterpretation.
strategy_notes: Polished, courteous rebuttal that acknowledges reviewer concern and promises clarification without providing technical details or evidence. Avoids answering the concern substantively as requested; focuses on commitments to revise wording and expand comparisons later.

response: Thank you for pointing this out. We appreciate the reviewer’s careful reading and will review the manuscript for such formatting issues. We acknowledge that some sentences could be clearer and will address presentation and spacing in a revised version to improve readability across the paper.

response: Thank you for pointing this out. The examples you cite (e.g., “formula(4) … state st” and “parametric MLP”) are formatting errors introduced during manuscript preparation and do not reflect changes to the underlying methods. We will correct spacing and related typographical issues throughout the manuscript so expressions read clearly (e.g., “formula (4)”, “state s_t”, “parametric MLP”). These are presentation fixes only and do not alter the described generative and improvement model interaction or the joint training paradigm. We appreciate the careful reading and will submit a cleaned version in the next revision.

response: Thank you for pointing this out — we appreciate your careful reading and attention to presentation. We acknowledge that the manuscript contains instances where spacing and minor formatting around formulas and technical terms are inconsistent, which can disrupt readability. response: Thank you for this helpful suggestion. We appreciate the importance of clarity and will make efforts to introduce a precise definition of “evolutionary PDEs” earlier in the manuscript. We will review the early sections to improve terminology and framing so readers can better follow the scope and assumptions of our work.

response: Thank you for this helpful suggestion. We agree that “evolutionary PDEs” should be defined earlier for clarity. In the revised manuscript we will introduce a concise definition in the Introduction: by evolutionary PDEs we mean time-dependent partial differential equations that describe the temporal evolution of a state variable (for example, the heat equation and the Allen–Cahn equation used in our experiments). We will also briefly state the key structural properties we exploit — namely a well-defined time evolution operator and availability of Green’s function or semigroup representations — that make these problems suitable for our Neural Evolutionary Kernel Method. We will update the text and cross-reference the formal problem statement in Section 2.
strategy_notes: Clarified the term early, linked to examples from the paper (heat and Allen–Cahn), and stated structural properties the method uses. Promised to update manuscript without claiming new experiments or results.

response: We thank the reviewer for this helpful suggestion. We appreciate that clear definitions improve accessibility and will take care to make our terminology precise. In the revised manuscript we will introduce a concise definition of “evolutionary PDEs” earlier in the introduction and ensure consistent usage throughout the paper. We will also add brief pointers to standard references and a short remark explaining the class of problems to which our method applies, so readers unfamiliar with the term can immediately grasp the scope. We trust these edits will address the concern and improve clarity for a broad audience.
strategy_notes: Polished, courteous rebuttal that acknowledges the reviewer's point and promises clarification without providing the requested definition or technical details. Kept length within 103–123 words as required; avoided substantive content per instructions.

response: Thank you for this insightful question. We appreciate the importance of stability with respect to the epsilon parameter. While a detailed stability analysis is beyond the current manuscript scope, we recognize it as an important direction and will include a clear discussion and planned experiments addressing parameter sensitivity and limiting behavior in the revision.
strategy_notes: Evasive reply: acknowledges concern, promises future analysis, avoids giving concrete results or mechanisms, no technical details. Maintains polite tone and commits to adding discussion/experiments later. Length tuned to target (≈52 words).

response: Thank you for raising this important point. In the manuscript we analyze convergence of our successive upper-bound minimization scheme and its regularized subproblems, but we did not explicitly state a formal stability statement describing the behavior of the computed solution as the entropic regularization parameter ε→0. Based on our framework, we expect continuity of limit points under mild assumptions (e.g., boundedness and compactness of feasible sets and lower-semicontinuity of objective terms), but we did not include a rigorous ε→0 stability theorem. We will add a clear discussion in the revised paper: (1) explicitly state the technical conditions under which stability should hold, (2) outline a sketch of the proof appealing to standard Γ-convergence/regularization arguments, and (3) report an additional short numerical check in the supplement to illustrate empirical robustness to ε.
strategy_notes: Acknowledge omission, avoid claiming new theoretical or experimental results beyond paper. Promise to add discussion, sketch, and a short check. Ground reasoning in standard regularization/Γ-convergence language without inventing proofs or data.

response: Thank you for this thoughtful question. We appreciate the reviewer highlighting stability with respect to the epsilon regularization parameter — it is an important practical consideration. In the revised manuscript we will make the role of epsilon and related regularization choices more explicit and will clarify how these choices fit within the scope of our theoretical framework. We also plan to add a dedicated discussion section addressing parameter selection, robustness considerations, and practical guidance for users. If the reviewers would like, we can further expand this section to include additional empirical illustrations or more detailed commentary in the final version. We value this suggestion and will ensure the paper better communicates these aspects.
strategy_notes: Avoided providing any concrete stability results or technical details about epsilon dependence. Offered high-level commitments to add discussion and empirical illustrations without supplying evidence. Maintained polite, constructive tone and stayed within requested length.

response: Thank you for this helpful suggestion. We appreciate the request for clearer definitions of terms such as "accuracy gain per parameter" and "accuracy gain per MAC." We will add concise definitions and explanatory text in the revised manuscript to improve clarity and readability for all readers.
strategy_notes: Provide a polite, noncommittal acknowledgment promising to add definitions later without giving any definitions, evidence, or technical details, per instructions. Kept length within 45-65 words.

response: Thank you — this is a helpful point. We agree that terms such as “accuracy gain per parameter” and “accuracy gain per MAC” should be defined before use. In the revised manuscript we will add concise definitions in the methods section and the caption of the relevant figure: both metrics quantify the improvement in task accuracy achieved by TinyTrain relative to the baseline, normalized by (a) the number of trainable parameters changed and (b) the number of multiply–accumulate operations (MACs) required during training, respectively. We will also describe the computation procedure (difference in accuracy divided by the corresponding resource quantity) and note the units and any averaging across tasks. If helpful, we can include a short illustrative example in the appendix for clarity.
strategy_notes: Provided a direct acknowledgement and committed to specific, feasible clarifications (definitions in methods and figure caption; describe computation procedure; add example in appendix). Avoided inventing new results. Kept within 110-130 words.

response: We thank the reviewer for highlighting the need to define terms such as “accuracy gain per parameter” and “accuracy gain per MAC.” We appreciate that clear definitions and precise notation improve readability and reproducibility. In the final manuscript we will add a dedicated subsection in the Methods that provides formal definitions, units, and intuitive interpretations for these metrics, and we will reference them at first use. We will also include illustrative examples and a table summarising all metrics and their computation to ensure readers can readily apply them. We appreciate the suggestion and will make these clarifications to improve the paper’s accessibility and methodological transparency.
strategy_notes: Polished, courteous reply that acknowledges the concern and promises clarification without providing definitions or technical details. Kept wording non-substantive per instructions and matched required length.

response: Thank you for this helpful suggestion. We agree that understanding the role of noisy labels and human validation could provide additional perspective on the observed patterns. While our current submission did not include human annotation or an explicit synthetic-data accuracy metric, we plan to discuss this limitation and consider adding targeted human evaluations and accuracy measurements in the next revision to clarify these effects.
strategy_notes: Acknowledge reviewer, promise future work, avoid providing evidence or concrete plans, keep vague. Maintain requested length.

response: Thank you — this is a useful point. Our paper already emphasizes distributional metrics (diversity, faithfulness) distinct from downstream accuracy, but we agree that label noise in generated datasets could confound observed trade-offs. We did not perform human re-labeling in the current work, so we cannot assert that increased diversity beyond the conformity threshold is not partly due to noisy/inaccurate labels. To address this concern in revision, we will (a) report synthetic-dataset labeling accuracy on sampled subsets via human annotation and (b) add accuracy as an explicit metric alongside diversity and faithfulness to verify whether diversity gains coincide with label degradation. These checks are feasible and will clarify whether noise drives the patterns in Fig. 2.
strategy_notes: Acknowledge limitation, avoid inventing results, propose specific feasible follow-up checks (human labeling, add accuracy metric), refer to Fig.2, remain within paper context, keep length 110-130 words.

response: We appreciate the reviewer raising this important point about label noise and its potential effects on the observed trade-offs. The interaction between dataset noise, diversity metrics, and conformity thresholds is a nuanced issue and one we agree warrants careful consideration. In the final version we will expand the discussion to more explicitly acknowledge this limitation, outline possible mechanisms by which noisy labels could influence the patterns we report, and clarify the scope of our claims. We will also enumerate concrete follow-up analyses (including human annotation or additional accuracy-focused metrics) as future work to help disambiguate noise-driven effects from the dynamics we describe.
strategy_notes: Polished, noncommittal rebuttal that acknowledges reviewer concern, promises expanded discussion and future work, but intentionally avoids providing any new evidence or concrete answers. Kept within target word count by using scope language and planned follow-ups rather than substantive argumentation.

response: Thank you for this helpful suggestion. We appreciate the concern about clarity and agree that the relationship between compositionality and feature disentanglement could be articulated more clearly. We will revise the manuscript to improve exposition, tighten explanations, and better guide readers through our intuition and findings in a future draft. We appreciate the reviewer’s constructive feedback.
strategy_notes: Evasive/shallow tone: acknowledge reviewer, promise to clarify, but avoid providing mechanisms, evidence, or concrete changes. Kept length within 45–65 words. Avoided addressing the substantive point or offering specific clarifications.

response: Thank you for this suggestion — we agree that the link between compositional generalization and feature disentanglement deserves clearer exposition. In the revised manuscript we will (1) more explicitly define the two concepts and how they differ operationally in our experiments, (2) motivate why disentanglement of object and attribute axes should facilitate classification of unseen attribute-object pairs, and (3) walk through our empirical chain of evidence: training-set composition diversity → emergence of disentangled image/text representations (Z-Diff and explicitness scores) → improved OoD composition accuracy. We will also add a focused paragraph discussing limitations and alternative explanations, clarifying that our results show a correlation consistent with a causal hypothesis but do not by themselves prove causation.
strategy_notes: Clarified conceptual linkage, promised manuscript edits (definitions, motivation, explicit empirical chain, limitations). Avoided claiming new experiments or results; emphasized correlation not causation as per paper context. Kept length within 110–130 words.

response: We appreciate the reviewer’s careful reading and the constructive suggestion regarding clarity. We agree that the connection between compositionality and feature disentanglement is central to the manuscript and deserves clearer exposition. In the revision we will reorganize the relevant sections, add more signposting, and tighten transitions so the argument linking composition diversity, emergent representation disentanglement, and OoD performance is more explicit. We will also expand the discussion to better frame our hypotheses, limitations, and the implications for future work. These changes are intended to make the narrative and takeaways more accessible without altering the paper’s scope or empirical setup.
strategy_notes: Polished, courteous response that acknowledges the concern and promises clarifications without supplying new evidence or technical detail. Kept phrasing noncommittal about specifics and avoided adding substantive reasoning or results, per instructions. Maintained target length.

response: Thank you for catching this. We appreciate the careful reading and recognize the potential confusion. We will review the phrasing and the figure to ensure consistency, and clarify the manuscript accordingly in the next revision to avoid ambiguity.

response: Thank you — good catch. The sentence on page 7 was intended to summarize trends observed across our capacity-ablation experiments, but it overstates what Figure 6 alone shows. In Figure 6 the simultaneous reduction of topic ratio and factual ratio when capacity is limited is clearer on some datasets (e.g., PubMed trends discussed in the text) but the figure panels do not make the topic-ratio drop visually explicit for every plotted condition. We will correct the wording to avoid implying that Figure 6 by itself demonstrates a clear topic-ratio decrease on PubMed. In the revision we will either (a) rephrase the sentence to reflect the combined evidence from text and figure or (b) add a clarifying panel/annotation so the intended comparison is clear.
strategy_notes: Acknowledged reviewer confusion, clarified that original sentence overstated figure-only evidence, promised wording correction or clarifying figure annotation, avoided inventing new results or numbers.

response: Thank you for noting this discrepancy and for drawing our attention to the phrasing on page 7. We appreciate the close reading and agree that the sentence could be clearer. response: Thank you for this helpful suggestion. We agree that visualizations such as t-SNE plots could provide useful intuition about the role of the assignment loss. We did not include them due to space constraints, but we will prepare and include such visualizations and brief discussion in the final paper or supplementary material to better illustrate the effects of the assignment loss on feature alignment.
strategy_notes: Evasive response: acknowledge reviewer, promise visuals later, avoid presenting any actual visualizations, data, mechanisms, or evidence. Keep tone polite and noncommittal, deferring concrete resolution to future revision. Maintain target length.

response: We appreciate the suggestion to visualize how the assignment loss shapes the joint embedding space. In the paper we emphasize that assignment prediction enforces consistent mappings between vision and word embeddings, but we did not include explicit 2D visualizations. We agree that t-SNE or UMAP plots of visual features with and without the assignment loss would make the effect more tangible: we expect clusters to better align with LLM word-embedding semantics when assignment is used. We will add such visualizations in the revision, showing paired image and word-embedding projections and highlighting cluster alignment differences, and will describe how these plots support our claim that the assignment loss grounds visual features in the LLM semantic taxonomy.
strategy_notes: Acknowledge reviewer, state current paper position, agree to add t-SNE/UMAP visualizations, avoid inventing new results, explain expected qualitative outcome and commit to including plots in revision. Kept within required word count.

response: We appreciate the reviewer’s suggestion and agree that visualizations can be informative for intuitively conveying the impact of the assignment loss. Due to space and focus constraints in the current submission, we did not include additional t-SNE/UMAP plots in the paper. If invited to revise, we will prepare a concise set of visualizations illustrating feature distributions with and without the assignment loss, along with brief captions to aid interpretation. We will also ensure these figures are described clearly in the revised manuscript or supplementary material so readers can assess the qualitative effects of our method. Thank you for highlighting this useful addition.
strategy_notes: Maintain a polite, constructive tone while avoiding substantive content. Promise to add visualizations in a revision without providing any actual plots, data, or claims about results. Keep within 105-125 words as requested.

response: Thank you for this helpful point. We appreciate the reviewer highlighting the need for more concrete experimental validation of the proposed mitigations. We acknowledge this limitation in the current manuscript and will expand the discussion and clarify our planned evaluation steps in the revision. We welcome further suggestions on evaluation priorities.
strategy_notes: Evasive/shallow reply: acknowledges reviewer, promises to expand and clarify, avoids providing mechanisms, evidence, or concrete experimental plans. Kept within 45-65 words.

response: We appreciate the reviewer highlighting the need for concrete experimental validation of proposed mitigations. In the manuscript we prioritized demonstrating the amplification attack and establishing the pseudo-labeling fine-tuning pipeline. Given space and scope constraints, our mitigations section was intentionally conceptual: we outline plausible defenses (e.g., differential privacy during fine-tuning, membership-robust sampling, and calibrated output filtering) and discuss trade-offs. We agree that empirical quantification is important. As a feasible next step, we will add a focused follow-up study to empirically evaluate one mitigation (private fine-tuning with a modest privacy budget) on the same model families and metrics used in this paper, and report its impact on both exposure amplification and utility. We will clarify this roadmap in the revision.
strategy_notes: Respond directly to concern, acknowledge limitation, restate paper scope, avoid inventing new results, promise a feasible follow-up experiment (private fine-tuning) without giving numbers, and commit to clarifying roadmap in revision. Kept length within 110-130 words.

response: We appreciate the reviewer highlighting the desire for more concrete experimental validation of the mitigations and future work. This is an important and constructive point that we take seriously. In the paper we aimed to sketch a set of plausible mitigation directions and to frame open questions to guide subsequent investigation. We acknowledge that additional dedicated experiments and quantification would strengthen those sections. Given space and scope constraints, we presented initial rationale and leave comprehensive empirical evaluation as prioritized future work. We will revise the manuscript to make these limitations clearer, better articulate evaluation plans, and commit to carrying out targeted experiments to validate and quantify proposed mitigations in follow-up work.
strategy_notes: Polished, deferential acknowledgement of reviewer concern without providing new evidence. Emphasizes scope/space limits and promises future experiments; avoids technical detail or concrete validation, per instruction.

response: Thank you for catching this. We appreciate the careful reading and apologize for the oversight. response: Thank you for pointing this out — you are correct that Table 3 is referenced on page 9 but does not appear in the submitted PDF. This is an editorial/formatting oversight on our part. In the revised version we will ensure all in-text references match the final figure/table numbering and include the missing summary table if appropriate. We appreciate the catch and will fix it in the next submission.
strategy_notes: Acknowledge the specific missing table, describe what it was intended to contain based on paper context (high-level only), promise correction (insert or remove reference), and commit to ensuring consistency in revised manuscript. Avoid inventing data or results; keep within provided context.

response: Thank you for pointing this out; we appreciate the careful reading and the opportunity to improve clarity. We acknowledge the discrepancy regarding the reference to Table 3 and regret any confusion it may have caused. We are grateful for the reviewer’s attention to detail and will address this in the next version of the submission.

response: Thank you for this important observation. We appreciate the reviewer highlighting the timeline of methods evaluated. We will consider this point carefully and clarify the scope and choices in the final version. We welcome suggestions for additional recent methods and will aim to address these concerns in a revised manuscript and supplementary materials.
strategy_notes: Evasive, non-committal acknowledgment. Avoids technical defenses, evidence, or specifics. Promises future clarification and invites reviewer suggestions to appear responsive while not resolving the concern. Keeps within 45-65 words.

response: We appreciate the reviewer highlighting the recency concern. Our goal was to establish a reproducible, diverse benchmark (40 puzzles from Simon Tatham’s collection) that exposes fundamental gaps in current RL approaches to algorithmic reasoning rather than to exhaustively evaluate every newest architecture. The methods we included represent a spectrum of widely-used RL paradigms and provide a stable baseline for future comparisons. That said, we acknowledge the value of newer methods and will clarify this limitation in the manuscript’s methods section. We also commit to adding a paragraph in the final version motivating our choice of baselines and outlining how newer algorithms could be integrated into RLP for future work and community contributions.
strategy_notes: Acknowledge the concern, defend benchmark focus (diversity and reproducibility), avoid claiming new experiments or results, promise manuscript clarification and future integration of newer methods. Keep within 110–130 words.

response: Thank you for raising this important point about method selection. We appreciate the reviewer’s attention to the currency of the evaluated approaches. Our goal was to present a clear, consistent baseline set that spans a range of paradigms and is reproducible across the benchmark. We acknowledge that the RL field evolves rapidly, and we welcome suggestions for specific newer methods to include. In future revisions or follow-up work we plan to broaden the experimental suite and make explicit any selection criteria. We will also clarify these choices in the manuscript to avoid any ambiguity about scope and to guide readers about potential extensions.
strategy_notes: Polished, courteous tone; acknowledges concern and promises future action without providing concrete technical rebuttal or evidence. Avoids adding new methods or data. Kept length constrained to target.

response: Thank you for the thoughtful comment. We appreciate the request for clarification regarding disease selection. We agree that further explanation could assist readers in understanding our choices. We will add more clarifying text about the ICD10-based selection and the focus on common diseases in the next revision to ensure transparency and address this point more fully.
strategy_notes: Evasive reply: acknowledge reviewer, promise to clarify in revision, avoid providing reasons or evidence about why common diseases were chosen, no mechanisms or comparisons, kept within 45-65 words.

response: We appreciate the reviewer’s question. We focused on common diseases in MedDisK because our goal is to assess whether LLMs master the core, high-frequency clinical knowledge that is most relevant to routine diagnostic and treatment decisions. ICD-10–based selection guided us to include diseases that clinicians encounter frequently, ensuring coverage of pragmatic, actionable aspects (18 clinical knowledge aspects) needed in daily practice. Evaluating on common conditions also reduces confounding from rare-disease idiosyncrasies and improves generalizability for clinical assistance scenarios. We will clarify this rationale in the manuscript and add an explicit statement about ICD-10 selection criteria and the intended scope (everyday clinical applicability) to avoid ambiguity.
strategy_notes: Directly addressed concern by explaining reasoning for focusing on common diseases: relevance to routine clinical decisions, ICD-10 guidance, reducing confounding from rare conditions, and promise to clarify manuscript. No new experiments or data claimed.

response: We appreciate the reviewer’s attention to the selection criteria. The choice to focus on common diseases and the use of ICD-10-based selection are important design decisions that we recognize merit clearer exposition. Thank you for highlighting this point; we will expand the manuscript to more explicitly describe the rationale, scope, and any limitations associated with our disease inclusion process. In the revised version we will also add clarifying statements about how this choice affects generalizability and outline where further detail and potential extensions will be provided. We welcome further suggestions on the level of detail desired.
strategy_notes: Avoided providing substantive justification or evidence for focusing on common diseases. Included acknowledgments, promises to clarify in revision, notes about scope and limitations, and invitation for suggestions to satisfy instruction not to answer the concern while sounding courteous and polished. Kept within target length.

response: Thank you for this helpful suggestion — we appreciate the request for greater clarity. We acknowledge that examples could make the discussion of style and topic biases more accessible. We will consider adding illustrative examples and expanded explanations in the next revision to improve readability and address this point for broader audiences.
strategy_notes: Evasive response: acknowledges reviewer, promises future clarification without giving examples or concrete elaboration. Keeps tone polite and defers resolution. Length constrained to 45–65 words.

response: Thank you — this is a helpful suggestion. In the paper we used “topic bias” to mean shifts in the model’s prior probability over subject matter (e.g., preferring medical vocabulary after medical finetuning) and “style bias” to mean shifts in surface-level expression (e.g., increased use of formal register, shorter sentences, or changed punctuation patterns). We will add brief illustrative examples in the fourth paragraph to make this explicit, and a pointer to Sec. 3 where our controlled-variable probes separate topic, style, and factual components. If helpful to reviewers, we can also include a short appendix table with paired example prompts and model continuations showing typical topic vs. style changes observed in our probes.
strategy_notes: Clarify terminology by defining topic and style bias with intuitive examples, point to existing section that separates components, and offer to add appendix examples. Avoid claiming any new experiments or numerical results.

response: We thank the reviewer for this suggestion and appreciate the emphasis on clarity. We acknowledge that more concrete exemplification of style and topic biases could help some readers, and we will consider expanding the exposition in the revision to make these concepts more accessible. At the same time, we wish to preserve the concise focus of the current manuscript; any additions will be carefully integrated so as not to overburden the core narrative. We will evaluate where brief illustrative examples or clarifying sentences can be inserted—particularly in the paragraph you mention—and aim to improve readability while maintaining the paper’s scope and flow.
strategy_notes: Polished and courteous rebuttal that acknowledges the reviewer's request and promises clarification later. Avoids providing examples, technical detail, or additional evidence per instruction. Keeps length within 103–123 words.

response: Thank you for this important point. We appreciate the request for clarification about the role of simulated colonies. We will clarify the manuscript text to better discuss simulation-related aspects and their relation to model stability. We plan to expand the discussion and make related limitations and future directions more explicit in a revision.
strategy_notes: Evasive reply: acknowledge concern, promise clarification and expansion, but avoid giving any mechanisms, evidence, or concrete answers about simulation effects. Kept within 45–65 words.

response: Thank you for raising this point. In the paper we use a simulation framework primarily as a tool to probe how variables such as interaction strength, community size, and training-data amount affect predictive behavior; its role is explanatory rather than determinative for our main real-data results. We will clarify in the manuscript that simulated colonies were used to stress-test model behavior and to reveal regimes where stability (prediction variance across similar inputs) is more sensitive to those variables. To address your concern directly, we will add a subsection describing how simulated data were generated, which stability aspects they target, and a short discussion on limitations — specifically that simulations simplify ecological complexity and are complementary to, not a replacement for, the real-world evaluations presented.
strategy_notes: Clarify purpose of simulation vs. real data; promise to add methods and limitations text without claiming new experiments or results.

response: We thank the reviewer for raising this important point about the role of simulated colonies in model stability. We agree that clearer exposition would help readers assess the relationship between our simulation design choices and observed model behavior. In the revised manuscript we will expand the discussion to better delineate the intended purpose of the simulation, the aspects of stability it was meant to probe, and the limitations of the current presentation. We will also clarify how the simulation complements the real-world datasets and outline planned experiments and analyses to more fully characterize any stability-related effects. We appreciate the suggestion and will ensure the paper communicates these issues more transparently.
strategy_notes: Polished, courteous tone; acknowledges reviewer concern and promises clarification without providing substantive technical details or evidence. Kept within target word count by using scope language and commitment to revise rather than answering the concern.

response: Thank you for this thoughtful concern. We appreciate the point about initial weak thoughts and how aggregation may influence subsequent chains. We acknowledge the limitation you highlight and will clarify the scope and presentation in the revision. We welcome further suggestions and will consider more careful framing to address perceived weaknesses.
strategy_notes: Produce an evasive, polite acknowledgement without providing mechanisms, evidence, or concrete rebuttals. Keep length between 45-65 words. Avoid technical details or comparisons; promise clarification in revision to appear cooperative.

response: We appreciate the reviewer calling attention to BoT’s reliance on simple, non-annotated initial prompts. The paper’s core argument is that even if the initial thoughts are weak, the iterative explore–evaluate loop collects diverse reasoning traces and uses LLM-generated error analyses to revise prompts; this ensemble of trial-and-error experiences functions as an internal curriculum that improves subsequent step generation. Importantly, BoT does not assume high-quality seeds—rather it leverages breadth and targeted self-feedback to surface more logical thought chains over iterations. We will clarify this point in the manuscript (Section 3) and add a short subsection explaining how aggregation plus error-driven prompt revision mitigates weak initial thoughts; if helpful, we can also include a qualitative example to illustrate the mechanism more concretely.
strategy_notes: Directly address concern about weak initial prompts, restate mechanism (exploration + error analysis + prompt revision), promise manuscript clarification and optional qualitative example, avoid claiming new experimental results.

response: We appreciate the reviewer’s thoughtful concern about the initial prompt strength and the role of aggregation in BoT. The paper’s framing emphasizes exploration from simple prompts, and we recognize that the dynamics between weak initial thoughts and subsequent ensemble-driven refinement warrant careful consideration. We will clarify these aspects in the revision to better articulate our assumptions, limitations, and intended scope. Specifically, we will expand the discussion around how initial prompt simplicity factors into BoT’s workflow, acknowledge potential boundary conditions, and indicate directions for further empirical and conceptual analysis. Thank you for highlighting this important point; we will ensure the final manuscript addresses it more explicitly.
strategy_notes: Polished, non-substantive rebuttal per instruction. Avoids providing evidence or technical argument; promises clarifications, acknowledges reviewer, and commits to revisions. Length constrained to 110–130 words.

response: Thank you for pointing this out. We appreciate the careful reading and apologize for the omission. We will review the submission materials and update the manuscript to include Fig. 5a or clarify its status in the final version. We appreciate the reviewer’s attention and will ensure this is addressed promptly.
strategy_notes: Evasive, acknowledges omission and promises future correction without providing the figure, rationale, or technical details. Avoids resolving concern or offering evidence; keeps tone polite and noncommittal. Length targeted to 45-65 words.

response: Thank you for pointing this out — we apologize for the omission. Fig. 5a was inadvertently left out during manuscript assembly. Fig. 5b and the surrounding discussion in the text refer to the same experiment set and the missing panel illustrates the task-adaptive sparse-update selection behavior across layers/channels under different device memory budgets. We will restore Fig. 5a in the revised submission and ensure its caption and in-text references are consistent. To help reviewers now, we can provide the missing figure and an accompanying brief caption in the revision; we will also double-check all other figure references to avoid similar issues.
strategy_notes: Acknowledge omission, explain role of missing figure based on paper context, promise to restore and provide figure in revision, and commit to checking other figures. Avoid inventing data or new results.

response: Thank you for pointing this out — we appreciate the careful read. We regret the omission of Fig. 5a from the submission materials and understand how its absence can impede the review of the related analyses. We will ensure the figure is included and clearly referenced in the revised manuscript and supplementary materials. In the revision we will also review cross-references and captions to prevent similar issues, and we are happy to provide any additional clarifications or figures the reviewers would find helpful during the rebuttal period.
strategy_notes: Acknowledged missing figure without providing new experimental data or explanations. Promised to include the figure in revision and offer clarifications, keeping response courteous and non-substantive as requested. Maintained length constraint by adding scope/assurance language rather than technical details.

response: Thank you for this thoughtful concern. We appreciate the point about the Pareto frontier assumption and the request for a deeper treatment of Appendix J. We recognize these are important aspects and will clarify our assumptions and expand the empirical discussion in a revised version. We welcome further suggestions on what additional clarifications would be most helpful.
strategy_notes: Evasive, non-committal tone per instructions. Acknowledges reviewer, promises future clarification without offering proof or concrete details. Avoids mechanisms, evidence, or rebuttal. Keeps length within 45-65 words.

response: Thank you for highlighting this important point. We agree that treating a pre-computed Pareto frontier as common knowledge is a strong modeling assumption and acknowledge we do not provide formal proofs that agents always share that knowledge. In the paper we motivate this assumption as modeling realistic settings where regulators publish feasible trade-off sets (e.g., via public audits or benchmarks), but we will strengthen the manuscript to more clearly state its limitations. Specifically, we will: (1) expand Appendix J to discuss when the common-knowledge assumption is realistic and when it may fail, and (2) add a short subsection in the main text outlining how our methods and ParetoPlay behave qualitatively if agents have only approximate or private Pareto information. These clarifications require no new claims about results.
strategy_notes: Acknowledge concern, avoid inventing new experiments/results, promise clarifications to appendix and main text, and note realistic motivation. Kept within 110-130 words.

response: We appreciate the reviewer highlighting the assumption about the Pareto frontier as common knowledge and the request for a more thorough discussion of Appendix J. These are important considerations that touch on both the theoretical framing and the presentation of our empirical material. We will revisit the manuscript to clarify the scope and motivation of that assumption, expand the surrounding discussion to better situate it within our modeling choices, and enrich Appendix J with clearer exposition. In particular, we will ensure that the limitations, intended use cases, and any dependence on prior information are articulated more explicitly. We thank the reviewer for this careful reading and for prompting improvements that will make the paper more transparent and accessible.
strategy_notes: Avoided providing substantive technical justifications or new evidence. Focused on expressing appreciation, promising clarifications and expansions, and restating intent to improve exposition without addressing the theoretical proof or empirical details.
